event_blocks.summary: On a calm rooftop overlooking a dark cityscape, a Boy with Binoculars, wearing a blue checkered shirt, uses green binoculars to observe the night sky. A Boy in Yellow Shirt, with short brown hair and glasses, approaches and asks in French, "Qu'est-ce que tu fais avec ces jumelles au milieu de la nuit?" The Boy with Binoculars replies in French, "J'essaie d'identifier les planètes du système solaire," explaining he has an oral exam on the solar system tomorrow. The Boy in Yellow Shirt then asks, "Dis-moi, tout d'abord, c'est quoi le système solaire?" The Boy with Binoculars defines it as "l'ensemble de soleil et les astres qui gravitent autour de lui, y compris notre planète," as light, whimsical background music continues.

Boy with Binoculars.description: A young boy with spiky black hair, wearing a blue checkered shirt over a white t-shirt, dark pants, and light-colored sneakers, holding green binoculars. He is initially focused on the sky, then turns to answer questions with a confident expression, speaking French with a clear, youthful voice to explain he's identifying planets for an oral exam and defining the solar system.

Rooftop Setting.description: A wooden rooftop with a white railing, overlooking a dark cityscape under a starry night sky, serving as the backdrop for the educational interaction, enhanced by light, whimsical background music.

Boy in Yellow Shirt.description: A boy with short brown hair, wearing glasses, a yellow polo shirt, brown cargo pants, and dark sneakers. He approaches the other boy, gestures while speaking, and acts as a tutor, asking questions about the solar system in French with a clear, youthful, and inquisitive voice.

Binoculars.description: Green handheld binoculars used by the boy in the blue checkered shirt to observe the night sky.

raw_segments: The video opens with a blue title screen displaying 'C Cube Academy' and an Instagram call-to-action, accompanied by light, whimsical background music. This transitions to a starry night sky, then to a calm rooftop setting with a wooden floor and white railing, overlooking a dark cityscape. A young boy with spiky black hair, wearing a blue checkered shirt, dark pants, and light sneakers, is seen using green binoculars to observe the sky. Another boy, with short brown hair, glasses, a yellow polo shirt, brown cargo pants, and dark sneakers, approaches him. With a clear, youthful voice, the boy in yellow asks in French, "Qu'est-ce que tu fais avec ces jumelles au milieu de la nuit?" (What are you doing with these binoculars in the middle of the night?), punctuated by a light sound effect. The boy with the binoculars, also speaking with a clear, youthful voice, confidently replies in French, "J'essaie d'identifier les planètes du système solaire" (I'm trying to identify the planets of the solar system), explaining that he has an oral exam on the solar system tomorrow. The boy in yellow, adopting a tutoring role, acknowledges this and then asks, "Dis-moi, tout d'abord, c'est quoi le système solaire?" (Tell me, first of all, what is the solar system?). The boy with the binoculars confidently defines it in French as "l'ensemble de soleil et les astres qui gravitent autour de lui, y compris notre planète" (the sun and all the celestial bodies that orbit around it, including our planet), as the background music continues to create an educational atmosphere.

event_blocks.summary: On a nighttime rooftop, the Boy in Yellow Shirt asks the Boy with Binoculars how many planets are in the solar system, to which he confidently replies, "There are 8." A starry space background appears, displaying the eight planets labeled in French, and the Boy with Binoculars recites their names: "Mercure, Vénus, Terre, Mars, Jupiter, Saturne, Uranus, et Neptune." When asked about their characteristics, the Boy with Binoculars admits he doesn't know, prompting the Boy in Yellow Shirt to invite him on a virtual journey to visit each planet. The Boy with Binoculars enthusiastically agrees, and a white and gray rocket with a blue nose cone appears on the rooftop with a "whoosh" sound. The rocket launches with a fiery blast and loud engine sound, beginning their adventure. They board the rocket, which launches with a powerful whoosh and yellow flames, carrying them into space. Their journey begins with Mercury, the closest and smallest planet to the Sun, with extreme temperature fluctuations and a year lasting 88 days. The rocket then propels them to Venus, the "twin planet" of Earth due to similar size and composition, though it was perhaps habitable in the past. Scientists believe Venus once had liquid water, but now it is the hottest planet at 460°C, with a rotation around the sun taking 225 Earth days. As they leave Venus and the Sun behind, the rocket accelerates towards Earth, which appears in the distance glowing with blue oceans and white clouds, accompanied by the sustained hum of the rocket engine.

Boy with Binoculars.description: A young boy with spiky black hair, wearing a blue checkered shirt over a white t-shirt, dark pants, and light-colored sneakers. He is seated on the Rocket, initially surprised by Venus’s 225-day year, then visibly shocked at its 460°C temperature, exclaiming "460 degrés Celsius? Oh mon Dieu!" in a surprised French voice, and finally excited as they transition to Earth, saying "Oui, on y va!" in an eager French voice.

Rooftop Setting.description: A wooden rooftop with a white railing, overlooking a dark cityscape under a starry night sky, serving as the initial backdrop before the rocket launches and the scene transitions to space.

Boy in Yellow Shirt.description: A boy with short brown hair, wearing glasses, a yellow polo shirt, brown cargo pants, and dark sneakers. He acts as the guide, calmly explaining Venus’s characteristics in a clear French voice, stating "En fait, les scientifiques pensent qu'il y avait de l'eau liquide à sa surface. Mais maintenant non," and later announcing the transition to Earth with a confident tone, "Et maintenant, c'est le tour de notre planète Terre."

Binoculars.description: Green handheld binoculars held by the Boy with Binoculars at the start, which he later puts down as he boards the rocket, no longer visible after the launch.

Rocket.description: A white and gray rocket with a blue nose cone and yellow flames, carrying the two boys through space. It moves steadily near the Sun and Venus, then accelerates with a distinct whooshing sound and a sustained engine hum as they leave Venus behind, heading toward Earth.

Mercury.description: A large, gray, cratered planet shown against a colorful nebula, with on-screen text indicating '88 jours' (88 days) to highlight its short year. It is visible at the start of the segment before the Rocket moves on.

Venus.description: A large, glowing, rocky planet with a fiery orange and yellow surface, shown in close proximity to the Sun. It is described as Earth’s twin and highlighted for its extreme heat (460°C) and 225-day orbit.

Sun.description: A massive, bright, orange-yellow star with visible solar flares and a glowing corona, dominating the background as the Rocket approaches Venus. It provides intense illumination and a warm, radiant atmosphere for the scene.

Earth.description: A large, blue and white planet with visible continents, oceans, and cloud cover, shown in the distance against a dark, star-filled space. It appears as the next destination, glowing softly as the Rocket approaches.

raw_segments: On a nighttime rooftop overlooking a dark cityscape under a starry sky, a young boy with spiky black hair, wearing a blue checkered shirt and holding green binoculars (Boy with Binoculars), stands beside a boy with short brown hair and glasses (Boy in Yellow Shirt). With light, inquisitive background music playing, the Boy in Yellow Shirt, speaking in French, asks, "Très bien. Et combien de planètes y a-t-il dans le système solaire?" (Very good. And how many planets are there in the solar system?). The Boy with Binoculars confidently replies, "Il y en a 8" (There are 8), followed by a positive chime sound. The scene then transitions to a starry space background displaying the eight planets, each labeled with its French name, which the Boy with Binoculars recites: "Mercure, Vénus, Terre, Mars, Jupiter, Saturne, Uranus, et Neptune." Another chime sounds as he finishes. Returning to the rooftop, the Boy in Yellow Shirt, with an encouraging tone, asks, "Excellent. Est-ce que tu connais les caractéristiques de chacune de ces planètes?" (Excellent. Do you know the characteristics of each of these planets?). The Boy with Binoculars admits with uncertainty, "Non, je ne sais pas" (No, I don't know). The Boy in Yellow Shirt reassures him, "Ce n'est pas grave" (It's not serious), and then, with a magical chime sound, excitedly invites him, "Veux-tu voyager virtuellement avec moi et visiter chacune d'elles?" (Do you want to travel virtually with me and visit each of them?). The Boy with Binoculars enthusiastically agrees, "Oui, bien sûr. Ça m'intéresse beaucoup!" (Yes, of course. That interests me a lot!). As he speaks, the Boy in Yellow Shirt gestures, and a white and gray rocket with a blue nose cone suddenly appears on the rooftop with a distinct "whoosh" sound. The rocket then launches with a fiery blast and a loud engine sound, initiating their virtual space adventure, all while the background music continues.

The scene opens on a rooftop at night, where the Boy in Yellow Shirt gestures excitedly toward a suddenly appearing Rocket, accompanied by a distinct whirring sound. He invites the Boy with Binoculars to board, saying in French, "Monte dans cette fusée." The Boy with Binoculars, initially holding his green binoculars, expresses awe at the rocket, exclaiming in French, "Waouh, une fusée ! C'est génial !" The Rocket then launches with a powerful whoosh and a burst of yellow flames, carrying both boys into the starry expanse of space, as gentle, adventurous background music begins. As they soar through the cosmos, the Boy in Yellow Shirt explains in French that their journey begins with the first planet of the solar system, Mercury, stating, "On commence par la toute première planète de notre système solaire, qui est Mercure." The Rocket approaches a large, cratered gray planet—Mercury—set against a vibrant nebula backdrop, accompanied by another whoosh sound. The Boy in Yellow Shirt continues to narrate in French, stating, "C'est la planète la plus proche du soleil et la plus petite," and that its temperature fluctuates extremely, "Sa température varie entre -173 et 427 degrés Celsius," displayed via a red thermometer graphic. He adds that a year on Mercury lasts 88 days, shown with a calendar icon. The Boy with Binoculars reacts to this information, saying in French, "C'est très instable," to which the Boy in Yellow Shirt replies, "Oui." Throughout, the boys remain seated on the Rocket, smiling and engaged, as the environment shifts from the urban rooftop to the vast, twinkling void of space, all underscored by the continuous adventurous music.

The scene opens with the Boy in Yellow Shirt and the Boy with Binoculars seated on their white and gray Rocket, floating in space near Mercury, a large, gray, cratered planet against a vibrant nebula. The Boy in Yellow Shirt, with a calm and educational tone, explains in French, "Et là-bas, une année dure 88 jours" (And there, a year lasts 88 days), referring to Mercury's 88-day year. The Boy with Binoculars, with an amazed voice, exclaims in French, "C'est incroyable!" (That's incredible!). The Rocket then propels forward with a distinct whooshing sound, leaving Mercury behind as the boys transition to their next destination. The environment shifts dramatically to a fiery, orange-hued space dominated by the massive, bright Sun, with Venus now appearing as a large, glowing, rocky planet. The Boy in Yellow Shirt announces in French, "Passons à notre deuxième planète, qui est Vénus" (Let's move to our second planet, which is Venus), and informs the Boy with Binoculars that Venus is the second closest planet to the Sun, adding, "Elle est appelée planète jumelle de la Terre" (It is called Earth's twin planet) due to their similar size and composition. The Boy with Binoculars, with a surprised and curious tone, asks in French, "C'est vrai? Je ne le savais pas. Et pourquoi?" (Is that true? I didn't know that. And why?). The Boy in Yellow Shirt calmly replies in French, "Tout simplement parce qu'elles sont semblables, de point de vue dimension et composition" (Simply because they are similar, from the point of view of size and composition). The Boy with Binoculars then inquires in French, "Donc, Vénus est habitable?" (So, is Venus habitable?), to which the Boy in Yellow Shirt responds in French, "Autrefois, peut-être qu'elle l'était" (In the past, perhaps it was), hinting at its potential for past habitability. Throughout, the Rocket continues to carry them smoothly through space, with the Sun’s intense glow illuminating the scene.

The scene opens with the Boy in Yellow Shirt and the Boy with Binoculars seated on the Rocket, now flying near the Sun, with Venus prominently displayed as a large, fiery orange planet in the foreground. The Boy in Yellow Shirt, speaking in a calm French voice, explains that scientists believe Venus once had liquid water on its surface, adding "Mais maintenant non" (But now no), a point emphasized by a subtle chime sound. The Boy with Binoculars, with a curious French voice, then asks about Venus’s orbital period, "De combien de temps cette planète a besoin pour faire une rotation autour du soleil?" (How much time does this planet need to make a rotation around the sun?). The Boy in Yellow Shirt answers, revealing it takes about 225 Earth days, accompanied by another chime. The Boy with Binoculars, still curious, asks, "Et quelle est sa température?" (And what is its temperature?). The Boy in Yellow Shirt continues, stating Venus is the hottest planet in the solar system, with a temperature of 460°C, which is visually emphasized by a digital thermometer graphic and a chime sound. The Boy with Binoculars reacts with shock, exclaiming "460 degrés Celsius? Oh mon Dieu!" (460 degrees Celsius? Oh my God!) in a surprised tone, followed by another chime. The Boy in Yellow Shirt then announces, "Et maintenant, c'est le tour de notre planète Terre" (And now, it's the turn of our planet Earth), signaling they are moving on to Earth, with the Boy with Binoculars excitedly responding, "Oui, on y va!" (Yes, let's go!). The Rocket accelerates with a distinct whooshing sound, leaving Venus and the Sun behind as the scene transitions to a dark, star-filled space with Earth appearing in the distance, glowing with blue oceans and white clouds, accompanied by the sustained hum of the rocket engine, signaling the next destination in their journey.

event_blocks.summary: The Rocket, emitting whooshing sounds, transported the Boy in Yellow Shirt and the Boy with Binoculars through space, first approaching Earth, a blue and white planet, where the Boy in Yellow Shirt explained its position as the third planet and its 365-day orbit around the sun, detailing the seasons with a ding sound. After the Boy with Binoculars expressed interest in Mars, they journeyed there, with the Rocket accelerating away from Earth amidst whirring and dinging. Upon reaching Mars, a reddish-brown planet, the Boy in Yellow Shirt identified it as the fourth planet with a 687-day year and temperatures ranging from -140°C to 30°C, explaining its 'Red Planet' moniker due to iron oxide in its soil. The journey then continued towards Jupiter, a massive gas giant with brown and white bands, where the Boy in Yellow Shirt noted it was the largest planet, composed of hydrogen and helium, and asked about its revolution. The Rocket then moved towards Saturn, a pale yellow planet with prominent rings, and the Boy in Yellow Shirt stated its 29.5 Earth-year orbit and temperatures between -189°C and -139°C, prompting the Boy with Binoculars to shiver. The next destination was Uranus, a pale blue ice giant, explained as being composed of solid water, ammonia, and methane, with an 84-year orbit and temperatures between -226°C and -197°C, leading to the Boy with Binoculars exclaiming it was too cold. Finally, they reached Neptune, a deep blue ice giant, where the Boy in Yellow Shirt mentioned its parts could orbit at different speeds and provided its temperature range of -218°C to -200°C, concluding the informational journey as the Boy with Binoculars expressed gratitude and the Rocket continued through the starry expanse.

Earth.description: A large, blue and white planet with visible continents, oceans, and cloud cover, shown in the distance against a dark, star-filled space. It appears as the next destination, glowing softly as the Rocket approaches and then recedes into the background, and is the subject of the Boy in Yellow Shirt's explanation.

Mars.description: A large, reddish-brown planet with visible surface features, set against a colorful nebula. Its year and temperature range are verbally explained in French, and its nickname 'the Red Planet' is discussed, with its red iron oxide soil composition detailed.

Boy with Binoculars.description: A young boy with spiky black hair, wearing a blue checkered shirt over a white t-shirt, dark pants, and light-colored sneakers. He is seated on the Rocket, speaking French, initially reacting with surprise to Uranus’s extreme cold by exclaiming, "Oh, il fait trop froid là-bas!", then listening intently as Neptune is explained, finding its unique orbital properties "étrange" (strange), and finally expressing gratitude with a smile, saying, "Merci beaucoup pour toutes ces informations intéressantes."

Boy in Yellow Shirt.description: A boy with short brown hair, wearing glasses, a yellow polo shirt, brown cargo pants, and dark sneakers. He acts as the guide, speaking French, calmly explaining Uranus’s temperature and composition, asking "Et quelle est sa température?", then confidently announcing the transition to Neptune, and continuing to explain Neptune’s unique orbital properties and temperature, gesturing as he speaks, and responding "Pas de problème."

Rocket.description: A white and gray rocket with a blue nose cone and yellow flames, carrying the two boys through space. It moves steadily near Uranus, then accelerates with a distinct whooshing sound as they leave it behind, heading toward Neptune, with the flames becoming more pronounced during the transition. A soft whirring sound accompanies its journey.

Digital Thermometer Graphic.description: A white, handheld digital thermometer with a blue screen, appearing next to Uranus and later Neptune to visually represent their respective temperature ranges, enhancing the educational content with a clear visual aid.

Jupiter.description: A massive gas giant with distinct brown and white bands, visible against a dark, star-filled space. It is introduced as having the shortest days but longest years, with swirling cloud patterns and a prominent Great Red Spot, emphasizing its size and gaseous composition.

29,5 années Sign.description: A white, notepad-style sign with red clips, displaying '29,5 années' in bold yellow text, floating above the Rocket to visually represent Saturn’s orbital period.

Saturn.description: A pale yellow gas giant with prominent, thick, multi-layered rings, visible against a dark, star-filled space. It is introduced as the sixth planet from the Sun, the second largest in the solar system, and famous for its beautiful ringlets, with the rings clearly visible and rotating slowly.

12 années Sign.description: A white, notepad-style sign with red clips, displaying '12 années' in bold yellow text, floating above the Rocket to visually represent Jupiter’s orbital period.

Uranus.description: A large, pale blue planet with a smooth, icy surface, set against a deep blue nebula. It is introduced as an ice giant, with a digital thermometer graphic appearing to show its temperature range of -226°C to -197°C.

84 ans Sign.description: A white, notepad-style sign with red clips, displaying '84 ans' in bold yellow text, floating above the Rocket to visually represent Uranus’s orbital period.

Neptune.description: A large, deep blue planet with subtle cloud bands, set against a vibrant, colorful nebula. It is introduced as an ice giant, with a digital thermometer graphic appearing to show its temperature range of -218°C to -200°C.

raw_segments: The scene opens with the Rocket, making whooshing sounds, carrying the Boy in Yellow Shirt and the Boy with Binoculars through space as they approach Earth, a large, blue and white planet. The Boy in Yellow Shirt, speaking in a clear, instructional male voice, explains that "Notre Terre est à la troisième position" (Our Earth is in the third position), followed by a ding sound. He continues, stating that "365 jours sont nécessaires à la Terre pour tourner autour du soleil" (365 days are necessary for Earth to turn around the sun), with a '365 jours' sign appearing on screen. The Boy with Binoculars, in a slightly higher-pitched, curious male voice, responds, "Oui, tu as raison. Je le sais." (Yes, you are right. I know it.) The Boy in Yellow Shirt then details the seasons, explaining that "Quand l'hémisphère nord est du côté du soleil, c'est l'été dans le nord et l'hiver dans le sud. Et vice-versa." (When the northern hemisphere is on the side of the sun, it's summer in the north and winter in the south. And vice-versa.), punctuated by another ding. The Boy with Binoculars finds this "intéressant" (interesting) and, showing excitement, asks, "Parle-moi de Mars." (Tell me about Mars.) The Boy in Yellow Shirt agrees, "Allons à Mars." (Let's go to Mars.), and with a whoosh and accelerating engine sounds, the Rocket rapidly accelerates, leaving Earth behind amidst high-pitched whirring and dinging. The scene transitions to Mars, a large, reddish-brown planet against a vibrant nebula. The Boy in Yellow Shirt, still guiding, states, "C'est la quatrième planète de notre système solaire." (It's the fourth planet of our solar system.), followed by a ding, as a '687 jours' sign appears, indicating Mars's year length. The Rocket continues its journey toward Mars through the star-filled expanse, with the boys engaged in conversation.

The scene opens with the Boy in Yellow Shirt and the Boy with Binoculars seated on the Rocket, now approaching Mars, a large, reddish-brown planet against a vibrant nebula. The Boy in Yellow Shirt, speaking in clear French, gestures toward Mars and explains that its year lasts 687 days, a fact confirmed by on-screen text and punctuated by a distinct chime sound. He continues, stating that Mars' temperature varies between -140°C and 30°C, accompanied by a digital thermometer graphic and another chime. He then explains that Mars is called 'the Red Planet,' prompting the Boy with Binoculars, also speaking in French, to curiously ask, "D'où vient ce nom?" (Where does this name come from?). The Boy in Yellow Shirt responds that its soil is composed mainly of red iron oxide, hence the name, with a final chime. With a whooshing sound and accelerating rocket engine noises, the Boy in Yellow Shirt announces it’s time to head for Jupiter, and the Rocket begins to move away from Mars. The background shifts from the colorful nebula to dark, star-filled space as the Rocket, with more intense engine sounds, approaches Jupiter, a massive gas giant with distinct brown and white bands. The Boy in Yellow Shirt, with a chime, informs the viewer that Jupiter is the largest planet in the solar system and is composed mostly of hydrogen and helium. He then asks about Jupiter’s revolution around the Sun, setting the stage for the next segment of their journey, all against a backdrop of subtle space-themed ambient sounds.

The scene opens with the Boy in Yellow Shirt and the Boy with Binoculars seated on the white and gray Rocket, now floating in space near Jupiter, a massive gas giant with swirling brown and white bands. The Boy in Yellow Shirt, speaking calmly, explains that although Jupiter’s days are the shortest, its years are very long, taking about 12 Earth years to revolve around the Sun, a fact visually reinforced by a floating '12 années' sign. The Boy with Binoculars reacts with a surprised, audible exclamation of "Oh, c'est trop long" (Oh, that's too long), to which the Boy in Yellow Shirt agrees, "Oui, c'est trop long" (Yes, that's too long). The Boy in Yellow Shirt then suggests, "Maintenant, allons à Saturne" (Now, let's go to Saturn), and the Rocket accelerates with distinct whooshing and engine sounds, its yellow flames becoming more pronounced as it leaves Jupiter behind. The scene transitions to Saturn, a pale yellow planet with prominent, thick rings. The Boy in Yellow Shirt continues his explanation, stating that Saturn is the sixth planet from the Sun and, audibly describing it as "la plus grande planète du système solaire" (the largest planet in the solar system) – a detail that contrasts with its visual description as the second largest – and famous for its beautiful thick ringlets. The Boy with Binoculars then asks, "En combien de temps elle orbite autour du soleil?" (How long does it orbit around the sun?), and the Boy in Yellow Shirt responds that Saturn needs "29,5 années terrestres" (29.5 Earth years) to orbit the Sun, as a '29,5 années' sign appears above. The Rocket remains near Saturn, with the vast, dark space and distant stars forming the backdrop.

The space journey continues with the Boy in Yellow Shirt and the Boy with Binoculars riding the Rocket through the cosmos, accompanied by whimsical background music. As they approach Saturn, a pale yellow gas giant with prominent, multi-layered rings, the Boy in Yellow Shirt explains that it takes 29.5 Earth years to orbit the Sun, a fact emphasized by a floating '29,5 années' sign. The Boy with Binoculars, in a curious, higher-pitched voice, exclaims "C'est très long!" and then asks, "Et quelle est sa température?" The Boy in Yellow Shirt, with a calm, explanatory tone, responds that it varies between -189°C and -139°C. The Boy with Binoculars visibly shivers, audibly stating, "Il gèle là-bas!" The Boy in Yellow Shirt then announces, "Passons à Uranus," and the Rocket accelerates with a distinct whooshing sound, leaving Saturn's rings in the distance. They approach Uranus, a large, pale blue ice giant set against a deep blue nebula. The Boy in Yellow Shirt explains, "Elle est considérée comme une géante de glace." The Boy with Binoculars, still curious, asks, "Et pourquoi?" The Boy in Yellow Shirt clarifies that its main components are water, ammonia, and methane in solid form. The Boy with Binoculars then inquires, "Et son temps de rotation?" (referring to its orbital period). The Boy in Yellow Shirt explains that Uranus takes "84 ans" to orbit the Sun, a fact highlighted by a floating '84 ans' sign, adding that "C'est l'espérance de vie moyenne" (the average human life expectancy), as the Boy with Binoculars listens attentively. Throughout the segment, subtle 'pop' or 'ding' sound effects accompany the appearance of signs or transitions.

The scene opens with the Boy in Yellow Shirt and the Boy with Binoculars, both speaking French, riding their white and gray Rocket through space, accompanied by a soft whirring sound. They approach Uranus, a large pale blue planet, where the Boy in Yellow Shirt asks, "Et quelle est sa température?" (And what is its temperature?), then explains that Uranus is an ice giant with a temperature varying between -226°C and -197°C, visually confirmed by a digital thermometer graphic. A "boing" sound punctuates the Boy with Binoculars' surprised reaction, exclaiming, "Oh, il fait trop froid là-bas!" (Oh, it's too cold there!). Another "boing" sound is heard as the Boy in Yellow Shirt confirms, "Oui," and announces, "Passons à notre dernière planète, Neptune." (Yes. Let's move on to our last planet, Neptune.) The Rocket then accelerates with a distinct whooshing sound, leaving Uranus behind. The scene transitions to Neptune, a deep blue planet set against a vibrant nebula. The Boy in Yellow Shirt continues his explanation, stating, "Elle est aussi considérée comme une géante de glace." (It is also considered an ice giant.) Another "boing" sound is heard. He further elaborates that since Neptune isn't entirely solid, "ses parties pourraient orbiter à des vitesses différentes." (its parts could orbit at different speeds.) A "boing" sound precedes the Boy with Binoculars' reaction, "Waouh, c'est étrange." (Wow, that's strange.) The Boy in Yellow Shirt then provides Neptune’s temperature range, "Sa température varie entre -218 et -200 degrés Celsius." (Its temperature varies between -218 and -200 degrees Celsius.), as a new digital thermometer graphic appears. Another "boing" sound is heard before the Boy with Binoculars expresses gratitude, "Merci beaucoup pour toutes ces informations intéressantes." (Thank you very much for all this interesting information.) The Boy in Yellow Shirt calmly responds, "Pas de problème." (No problem.) The Rocket continues its journey through the starry expanse, with the two boys seated calmly, observing the distant planets, accompanied by the ongoing whirring sound.

event_blocks.summary: The Boy in Yellow Shirt and the Boy with Binoculars are seated on a white and gray Rocket, traversing a star-filled space with a large blue planet in the background, accompanied by cheerful music. The Boy in Yellow Shirt, wearing glasses and a yellow polo, confidently speaks in French, saying "Il n'y a pas de quoi!" and asking, "Maintenant je vais te poser une question, es-tu prêt?" The Boy with Binoculars, in a blue checkered shirt, responds with "Oui." A 'ding' sound effect accompanies the Boy in Yellow Shirt's question, "Quelle planète est surnommée « La planète Rouge ?" The Boy with Binoculars, smiling, confidently answers in French, "Oui, je le sais! C'est la planète 'Mars'." followed by another 'ding.' The Boy in Yellow Shirt praises him, "Très bien. Tu as tout à fait raison," with a final 'ding.' On-screen text in French and English mirrors the dialogue. The Rocket continues to emit bright yellow flames. The Boy in Yellow Shirt then encourages viewers in French to "Si vous avez aimé cette vidéo, appuyez sur le bouton « J'aime »" and "Et pour de nouvelles vidéos intéressantes, n'oubliez surtout pas de vous abonner à notre chaîne!" The Boy with Binoculars adds, "Merci de votre soutien! À la prochaine!" The Rocket continues its journey through the starry expanse, with the boys smiling and waving as the scene fades out, while the CUBE ACADEMY Logo remains visible in the top right corner.

Boy with Binoculars.description: A young boy with spiky black hair, wearing a blue checkered shirt over a white t-shirt, dark pants, and light-colored sneakers. He listens intently, then confidently answers questions in French, smiling proudly, and later waves as they conclude the video, also thanking viewers for their support.

CUBE ACADEMY Logo.description: A yellow and blue logo in the top right corner, reading 'CUBE ACADEMY' in bold, stylized text, remaining consistently visible throughout the segment as the channel's branding.

Boy in Yellow Shirt.description: A boy with short brown hair, wearing glasses, a yellow polo shirt, brown cargo pants, and dark sneakers. He acts as the guide, speaking in a clear, confident French voice, gesturing with his hand as he asks questions, praises the other boy, and finally encourages viewers to like and subscribe, maintaining a calm and engaging demeanor throughout.

Rocket.description: A white and gray rocket with a blue nose cone and bright yellow flames, carrying the two boys through space. It moves steadily through the starry background, with the flames becoming more pronounced as it propels forward, serving as their vehicle for the educational journey.

raw_segments: The scene opens with the Boy in Yellow Shirt and the Boy with Binoculars seated on a white and gray Rocket, flying through a star-filled space with a large blue planet visible in the background, accompanied by cheerful background music. The Boy in Yellow Shirt, wearing glasses and a yellow polo, confidently speaks in French, gesturing as he says, "Il n'y a pas de quoi!" (No problem!) and then asks, "Maintenant je vais te poser une question, es-tu prêt?" (Now I'm gonna ask you a question, are you ready?). The Boy with Binoculars, in a blue checkered shirt, listens attentively and responds with a simple "Oui." A 'ding' sound effect plays as the Boy in Yellow Shirt then poses the question, "Quelle planète est surnommée « La planète Rouge ?" (Which planet is nicknamed "The Red Planet"?). The Boy with Binoculars, smiling proudly, confidently answers in French, "Oui, je le sais! C'est la planète 'Mars'." (Yes, I know! It is the planet 'Mars'.), followed by another 'ding' sound. The Boy in Yellow Shirt praises him, stating, "Très bien. Tu as tout à fait raison." (Very well. You're absolutely right.), with a final 'ding'. On-screen text appears in both French and English, mirroring the dialogue. The Rocket continues to emit bright yellow flames as it moves forward. The Boy in Yellow Shirt then transitions to a call to action, encouraging viewers in French to "Si vous avez aimé cette vidéo, appuyez sur le bouton « J'aime »." (If you liked this video, hit the "Like" button.) and "Et pour de nouvelles vidéos intéressantes, n'oubliez surtout pas de vous abonner à notre chaîne!" (And for new interesting videos, don't forget to subscribe to our channel!). The Boy with Binoculars joins in, adding, "Merci de votre soutien! À la prochaine!" (Thank you for your support! See you next time!). The Rocket continues its journey through the dark, starry expanse, with the boys smiling and waving as the scene fades out, while the CUBE ACADEMY Logo remains visible in the top right corner.

event_blocks.summary: A white and gray Rocket, distinguished by its blue nose cone and bright yellow flames, accelerates through a starry void with a pronounced whooshing sound, emitting a vivid yellow trail before departing the visual field, while the CUBE ACADEMY logo remains fixed in the upper right quadrant. Subsequently, an upbeat, rhythmically Latin-infused instrumental music piece begins and persists as the scene shifts to a vibrant yellow backdrop featuring the word 'COMMENTS' adorned with five pink stars. A white comment box materializes, sequentially displaying viewer contributions in French, Portuguese, and Spanish, which commend the video's educational merit, narrative construction, and elocution, with specific usernames such as 'Wendy Hlatshwayo' and 'The Wondering Astronaut' being visible, alongside interactive elements like likes, hearts, and reply functionalities. The sequence culminates in a soft pink gradient End Card, animated with shimmering sparkles and a revolving green ring, presenting the messages 'THANKS FOR WATCHING!', 'WATCH MORE', a red 'SUBSCRIBE' button, and the directive 'DON'T FORGET TO LIKE, COMMENT AND SUBSCRIBE!', all underscored by the continuous upbeat musical score.

Comments Section.description: A white rectangular box on a yellow background displaying viewer comments in multiple languages, with usernames, timestamps, likes, hearts, and reply options, cycling through different sets of feedback, all set to an upbeat, rhythmic instrumental music track.

End Card.description: A soft pink gradient background with animated sparkles and a rotating green ring, featuring bold black text 'THANKS FOR WATCHING!', 'WATCH MORE' on both sides, a red 'SUBSCRIBE' button, and the phrase 'DON'T FORGET TO LIKE, COMMENT AND SUBSCRIBE!' below, accompanied by an upbeat, rhythmic instrumental music track.

CUBE ACADEMY Logo.description: A yellow and blue logo in the top right corner, reading 'CUBE ACADEMY' in bold, stylized text, remaining consistently visible throughout the segment as the channel's branding.

Rocket.description: A white and gray rocket with a blue nose cone and bright yellow flames, shown moving through space before exiting the frame, serving as the vehicle for the boys' journey, accompanied by a distinct whooshing sound.

raw_segments: The segment begins dynamically with a white and gray Rocket, featuring a blue nose cone and bright yellow flames, speeding through a starry expanse with a distinct whooshing sound, leaving a bright yellow trail before exiting the frame. The CUBE ACADEMY logo is consistently present in the top right. An upbeat, rhythmic instrumental music track, with a slightly Latin flavor, then commences and plays continuously as the video transitions to a bright yellow background displaying 'COMMENTS' with five pink stars. A white comment box appears, cycling through viewer feedback in French, Portuguese, and Spanish, praising the video's educational value, storytelling, and pronunciation, with usernames like 'Wendy Hlatshwayo' and 'The Wondering Astronaut' highlighted alongside visible likes, hearts, and reply options. The segment concludes with a soft pink gradient End Card, animated with sparkles and a rotating green ring, displaying 'THANKS FOR WATCHING!', 'WATCH MORE', a red 'SUBSCRIBE' button, and the phrase 'DON'T FORGET TO LIKE, COMMENT AND SUBSCRIBE!', all accompanied by the ongoing upbeat music.

global_entity_library.Boy with Binoculars: A young boy with spiky black hair, wearing a blue checkered shirt over a white t-shirt, dark pants, and light-colored sneakers. He listens intently, then confidently answers questions in French, smiling proudly, and later waves as they conclude the video, also thanking viewers for their support.
global_entity_library.Rooftop Setting: A wooden rooftop with a white railing, overlooking a dark cityscape under a starry night sky, serving as the initial backdrop before the rocket launches and the scene transitions to space.
global_entity_library.Boy in Yellow Shirt: A boy with short brown hair, wearing glasses, a yellow polo shirt, brown cargo pants, and dark sneakers. He acts as the guide, speaking in a clear, confident French voice, gesturing with his hand as he asks questions, praises the other boy, and finally encourages viewers to like and subscribe, maintaining a calm and engaging demeanor throughout.
global_entity_library.Binoculars: Green handheld binoculars held by the Boy with Binoculars at the start, which he later puts down as he boards the rocket, no longer visible after the launch.
global_entity_library.Rocket: A white and gray rocket with a blue nose cone and bright yellow flames, shown moving through space before exiting the frame, serving as the vehicle for the boys' journey, accompanied by a distinct whooshing sound.
global_entity_library.Mercury: A large, gray, cratered planet shown against a colorful nebula, with on-screen text indicating '88 jours' (88 days) to highlight its short year. It is visible at the start of the segment before the Rocket moves on.
global_entity_library.Venus: A large, glowing, rocky planet with a fiery orange and yellow surface, shown in close proximity to the Sun. It is described as Earth’s twin and highlighted for its extreme heat (460°C) and 225-day orbit.
global_entity_library.Sun: A massive, bright, orange-yellow star with visible solar flares and a glowing corona, dominating the background as the Rocket approaches Venus. It provides intense illumination and a warm, radiant atmosphere for the scene.
global_entity_library.Earth: A large, blue and white planet with visible continents, oceans, and cloud cover, shown in the distance against a dark, star-filled space. It appears as the next destination, glowing softly as the Rocket approaches and then recedes into the background, and is the subject of the Boy in Yellow Shirt's explanation.
global_entity_library.Mars: A large, reddish-brown planet with visible surface features, set against a colorful nebula. Its year and temperature range are verbally explained in French, and its nickname 'the Red Planet' is discussed, with its red iron oxide soil composition detailed.
global_entity_library.Digital Thermometer Graphic: A white, handheld digital thermometer with a blue screen, appearing next to Uranus and later Neptune to visually represent their respective temperature ranges, enhancing the educational content with a clear visual aid.
global_entity_library.Jupiter: A massive gas giant with distinct brown and white bands, visible against a dark, star-filled space. It is introduced as having the shortest days but longest years, with swirling cloud patterns and a prominent Great Red Spot, emphasizing its size and gaseous composition.
global_entity_library.29,5 années Sign: A white, notepad-style sign with red clips, displaying '29,5 années' in bold yellow text, floating above the Rocket to visually represent Saturn’s orbital period.
global_entity_library.Saturn: A pale yellow gas giant with prominent, thick, multi-layered rings, visible against a dark, star-filled space. It is introduced as the sixth planet from the Sun, the second largest in the solar system, and famous for its beautiful ringlets, with the rings clearly visible and rotating slowly.
global_entity_library.12 années Sign: A white, notepad-style sign with red clips, displaying '12 années' in bold yellow text, floating above the Rocket to visually represent Jupiter’s orbital period.
global_entity_library.Uranus: A large, pale blue planet with a smooth, icy surface, set against a deep blue nebula. It is introduced as an ice giant, with a digital thermometer graphic appearing to show its temperature range of -226°C to -197°C.
global_entity_library.84 ans Sign: A white, notepad-style sign with red clips, displaying '84 ans' in bold yellow text, floating above the Rocket to visually represent Uranus’s orbital period.
global_entity_library.Neptune: A large, deep blue planet with subtle cloud bands, set against a vibrant, colorful nebula. It is introduced as an ice giant, with a digital thermometer graphic appearing to show its temperature range of -218°C to -200°C.
global_entity_library.CUBE ACADEMY Logo: A yellow and blue logo in the top right corner, reading 'CUBE ACADEMY' in bold, stylized text, remaining consistently visible throughout the segment as the channel's branding.
global_entity_library.Comments Section: A white rectangular box on a yellow background displaying viewer comments in multiple languages, with usernames, timestamps, likes, hearts, and reply options, cycling through different sets of feedback, all set to an upbeat, rhythmic instrumental music track.
global_entity_library.End Card: A soft pink gradient background with animated sparkles and a rotating green ring, featuring bold black text 'THANKS FOR WATCHING!', 'WATCH MORE' on both sides, a red 'SUBSCRIBE' button, and the phrase 'DON'T FORGET TO LIKE, COMMENT AND SUBSCRIBE!' below, accompanied by an upbeat, rhythmic instrumental music track.
